title: Godley-Lavoie Fiscal Policy SFC Model (Levy WP 494)

cells.title: Overview
cells.source:
# Fiscal Policy in a Stock-Flow Consistent Model

This notebook implements the simple growth economy from Godley and Lavoie (2007),
[*Fiscal Policy in a Stock-Flow Consistent (SFC) Model*](https://www.econstor.eu/bitstream/10419/31585/1/571694225.pdf)
(Levy Economics Institute Working Paper No. 494; also [Levy WP 494](https://www.levyinstitute.org/pubs/wp_494.pdf)).

The model has three sectors — **households** (private), **firms** (aggregated with households in the accounting matrix), and **government** — and a single financial asset: **government debt** held as private wealth.

Private decisions are taken in **real** terms; government policy (tax rate, interest rate) is **nominal**. Pure government spending `g` is endogenous: it fills the gap between full-employment output and private expenditure, so the fiscal stance sustains growth at the potential rate `gr`.

Section 1 of the paper (closed economy) is the baseline. Section 2 adds a fiscal policy reaction function with a Phillips curve (Figures 2–8). Section 3 adds a rudimentary foreign sector via `trade_gap` and foreign-wealth accumulation `VF` (Figure 9). Scenarios reproduce the paper's main experiments: a high interest-rate shock (Figure 1), fiscal-policy responses to inflation and spending shocks (Figures 2–8), and a permanent trade deficit (Figure 9).
cells.more:
Godley & Lavoie's Levy WP 494 (_Fiscal Policy in a Stock-Flow Consistent Model_,
April 2007) argues against the _New Consensus_ view that monetary policy alone can
deliver full employment and stable inflation. In their simple SFC growth model, the
**fiscal stance** — real government outlays relative to the tax rate,
`(g + rr * gd) / theta` — must be set at the right **level and growth rate** if the
economy is to grow at potential with low inflation.

Three results stand out:

- **Endogenous pure spending.** Equation (21) makes real goods-and-services spending
  `g` fill the gap between full-employment output `y` and private expenditure `x`
  (plus net exports when the foreign sector is open). Fiscal policy is not an
  afterthought; it _closes the real resource balance_ every period.
- **A surprising debt dynamic.** Conventional wisdom says debt explodes when the
  after-tax real interest rate exceeds the growth rate. In this model, with fiscal
  policy sustaining full employment, higher rates raise `gd/y` but the real deficit
  ratio **converges** rather than exploding (Figure 1).
- **Open-economy balances.** A chronic trade deficit raises the budget deficit needed
  for the same domestic outcome: identity (D) requires
  `NAFA = DEFICIT + CAB` (Section 3, Figure 9).

This notebook implements **Section 1** (closed economy, Table 1–2) as the baseline,
**Section 2** (fiscal reaction function, Figures 2–8), and **Section 3** (open economy,
Figure 9).

cells.title: Transactions-flow matrix (Table 1)
cells.note: Real government expenditure on goods and services is endogenous; interest flows use nominal debt stocks.
cells.more:
This is **Table 1** in WP 494 — the closed-economy transactions-flow matrix. Every
payment is simultaneously a receipt, so each **row and each column sums to zero**.

- **Private expenditure** `X` leaves households and enters firms as sales.
- **Government expenditure on goods** `G` is the nominal counterpart of endogenous
  real spending `g`.
- **GDP** `Y` is private factor income received by households and paid out by firms.
- **Taxes** `T` are levied on nominal income plus interest receipts at rate `theta`.
- **Interest** `r * lag(GD)` flows from government to bondholders (households).

The matrix uses **nominal** flows at current prices. Real decisions — consumption,
wealth targets, the fiscal gap — are defined in the equation block below and mapped
to nominal variables through the price level `p`.

The paper's redundant accounting check in the closed economy is
`NAFA = DEFICIT`: private net acquisition of financial assets equals the government
budget deficit. The solver watches this through hidden variables `NAFA` and
`public_balance`.

cells.title: Transaction flow sequence
cells.description: Sequence view generated from the WP 494 transactions-flow matrix.
cells.more:
Read the matrix **column by column** as each sector's budget constraint. The
household column shows how factor income, taxes, interest receipts, and saving
(`NAFA`) line up; the government column shows spending, taxes, interest payments,
and debt issuance (`DEFICIT`). This is the same quadruple-entry logic as in
_Monetary Economics_, applied to a growing economy with interest-bearing debt
rather than pure government money.

cells.title: WP 494 fiscal policy model

equations.name: g
equations.desc: Real pure government spending — fiscal stance (eq. 21 / 21-2).

equations.name: gT
equations.desc: Real total government outlays incl. interest (eq. 15).

equations.name: gd_y
equations.desc: Real government debt to real GDP.

cells.more:
The model splits cleanly into a **real private block** and a **nominal government
block**, linked by the price level and Fisher relation `rr = (1 + r) / (1 + PI) - 1`.

**Private sector (real).** Disposable income `yd = y + rr * lag(v) - t` (eq. 1)
includes after-tax interest on wealth. Spending follows a wealth-adjustment form:
target wealth `v_star = alpha3 * yd` (eq. 5), partial adjustment
`v = lag(v) + alpha2 * (v_star - lag(v))` (eq. 4), and expenditure
`x = yd - (v - lag(v))` (eq. 7). The propensity to spend from income
`alpha1 = alpha10 - iota * lag(rr)` (eq. 3) falls when the real rate rises.

**Fiscal closure.** Pure government spending is **endogenous**:
`g = y - x - trade_bal` (eq. 21 / 21-2). Given tax rate `theta` and full-employment
output growing at `gr`, the government must spend enough on goods to absorb whatever
private demand and net exports leave unused. Debt accumulates from the real deficit
`gd = I(gT - t)` where `gT = g + rr * lag(gd)` (eqs. 15–16).

**Open economy (Section 3).** Exports grow at `gr`; imports are a fixed fraction
`trade_gap` of exports. With `trade_gap = 1.05` and `ex/y = 20%`, the goods trade
deficit is `0.05 × 20% = 1%` of GDP. The current account `CAB = EX - IM + r * lag(VF)`
and foreign wealth `VF = I(CAB)` close the external sector. Identity (D) generalizes to
`NAFA = DEFICIT + CAB`.

**Steady-state anchors (Table 2, baseline).** With `gr = 2.5%`, `theta = 25%`,
`r = 3%`, `PI = 2%`, the paper reports `g/y = 25.9%`, `DEFICIT/Y = 1.8%`,
`deficit_y` (`Δgd/y`) `= 1.0%`, and `gd/y = 40.9%` in the growing steady state.

cells.title: Solver
cells.more:
The model is solved period by period with lagged stocks known from the previous
period. The redundant closed-economy identity `NAFA = DEFICIT` (open economy:
`NAFA = DEFICIT + CAB`) is **not** imposed as a solving equation; instead the solver
checks that `NAFA` matches `public_balance` (`DEFICIT + CAB`) within
`hiddenTolerance`. If the flow-of-funds accounting leaked, this check would fail —
the numerical counterpart of Godley & Lavoie's watertight SFC tables.

cells.title: Externals

externals.name: gr
externals.desc: Real growth rate of productive potential (2.5%).

externals.name: alpha2
externals.desc: Speed of wealth adjustment / propensity out of wealth.

externals.name: alpha10
externals.desc: Autonomous propensity to spend from income.

externals.name: trade_gap
externals.desc: Import-to-export ratio; 1.05 gives a 5% import excess → 1% of GDP trade deficit when ex/y = 20% (Section 3).

cells.more:
These are the **Table 2 baseline** parameters from WP 494 (first column):

- `gr = 0.025` — real growth of productive potential (2.5% per year).
- `theta = 0.25` — tax rate on income plus interest receipts.
- `alpha2 = 0.2` — speed of wealth adjustment / propensity to spend out of wealth.
- `alpha10 = 0.9` — autonomous propensity to spend from income.
- `iota = 0.2` — sensitivity of `alpha1` to the real interest rate (eq. 3).
- `inflation = 0.02` — exogenous inflation `PI` (2%).
- `r = 0.03` — nominal interest rate (3%); implies `rr` just under 1%.
- `trade_gap = 1` — balanced trade in the baseline (`im = ex`); set to `1.05` in
  Scenario 2 for a 5% import excess.

Policy levers `r` and `theta` are nominal; the behavioral core reacts through the
Fisher real rate `rr` and the tax-deflated flows `t` and `yd`.

cells.title: Initial values
cells.more:
The run starts from a configuration consistent with the paper's growing steady
state: real output and exports at `100` and `20` (`ex/y = 20%`, so a `5%` import
excess yields a `1%` goods trade deficit), price level `1`, and government /
private real wealth stocks `gd` and `v` at `41` (roughly `41%` of initial `y`).
`VF = 0` — no foreign assets or liabilities until Scenario 2 opens a trade gap.

cells.title: Baseline
cells.description: Closed-economy growing steady state (Section 1, Table 2 baseline parameters).
cells.more:
The baseline integrates **Section 1** forward for 80 periods with balanced trade
(`trade_gap = 1`). Output `y` grows at `gr = 2.5%`; the price level grows at
`PI = 2%`. Endogenous `g` adjusts each period so that `y = x + g`.

In the paper's Table 2 steady state, pure government spending must reach
**`g/y = 25.9%`** to sustain full-employment growth, with a nominal deficit ratio
**`DEFICIT/Y = 1.8%`** and real deficit ratio **`deficit_y = 1.0%`**. The notebook
run at period 80 shows the same orders of magnitude (`g_y` near `0.26`,
`DEFICIT_Y` near `0.018`). Debt-to-GDP `gd_y` continues adjusting toward the
paper's `40.9%` steady-state target over a longer horizon.

cells.title: Baseline fiscal ratios
cells.more:
Watch the **fiscal ratios** that Table 2 organizes: `g_y` (pure spending share),
`deficit_y` (real deficit / GDP), `gd_y` (debt / GDP), and `DEFICIT_Y` (nominal
deficit / GDP). In the baseline they flatten as the economy approaches the growing
steady state described in WP 494.

Real output `y` and endogenous spending `g` both grow at `gr`, but nominal flows
grow faster because of inflation. The fiscal stance ratio `(g + rr * gd) / theta`
must grow at `gr` once full employment is achieved — the paper's rule for a
sustainable fiscal setting.

cells.title: Baseline summary (period 80)
cells.more:
Use this table to read off period-80 levels against Table 2. Key checks:

- `rr` just under `1%` and `alpha1` near `0.88` match the paper's first column.
- `g_y` near **`25.9%`** — the endogenous spending share needed for full employment.
- `DEFICIT_Y` near **`1.8%`** and `deficit_y` near **`1.0%`** — deficits are
  _required_, not a sign of instability, in a growing steady state with positive
  real interest rates.
- `fiscal_stance` summarizes `(g + rr * gd) / theta` — the paper's summary policy
  indicator.

cells.title: Scenario 1 — High interest rate
cells.source:
Reproduces Figure 1: the nominal interest rate `r` rises from 3% to 10% in period 10.
Even when the after-tax real rate exceeds the growth rate, the model converges to a
higher but stable debt-to-GDP ratio — the paper's "surprising result."
cells.more:
**Figure 1 experiment (Section 1).** The paper pushes the nominal rate from `3%` to
`10%`, raising the real rate `rr` to about **`7.8%`** — far above the `2.5%` growth
rate and above the after-tax real rate **`(1 - theta) * rr ≈ 5.9%`**. Conventional
debt-sustainability rules would demand ever-larger primary surpluses.

Instead, with fiscal policy still closing the output gap through endogenous `g`, the
real deficit ratio **`deficit_y`** and debt ratio **`gd_y`** **converge** to new
steady levels (Table 2 last column: `gd/y` toward **`93.5%`** in the long run). The
notebook shock from period 10 shows `gd_y` rising above the baseline and `rr`
jumping to about `7.8%` — the transition toward the higher-debt steady state, not an
explosion.

cells.title: High interest rate shock
cells.more:
From period 10, `r` is held at **`10%`**. Higher interest receipts raise disposable
income and initially support spending, but `alpha1` falls through eq. (3), and the
fiscal authority must raise `g` to keep `y` on its full-employment path. Debt service
rises, so **`gd_y`** and **`DEFICIT_Y`** move up relative to the baseline. The paper
stresses that this adjustment is **stable** even when `rr` greatly exceeds `gr`.

cells.title: Figure 1 — deficit and debt ratios after rate shock
cells.more:
Compare **`deficit_y`** and **`gd_y`** against the baseline reference trace. After the
rate step, both ratios rise and then level off — the signature of Figure 1. Also watch
`rr` and `r`: the Fisher relation links the nominal shock to the real rate faced by
households in `yd` and by the government in `gT`.

The paper's broader policy point: fixed Maastricht-style deficit or debt ceilings
ignore this mechanism. Higher rates _require_ higher deficit ratios if full employment
is to be preserved through the fiscal stance.

cells.title: Section 2 — Fiscal policy reaction function
cells.source:
Section 2 endogenizes inflation through a vertical Phillips curve (eq. 26) and replaces
the fixed full-employment output path with demand-led output `y = g + x` alongside
potential output `ys`. Pure government spending follows a **fiscal reaction function**
(eqs. 27–28): the growth rate of `g` responds to inflation and the inflation target.
The central bank keeps the **real** interest rate `rr` constant (eq. 11-2), adjusting
the nominal rate `r` as inflation moves.

Three experiments reproduce **Figures 2–8**: a lower inflation target, a higher
propensity to consume, and a jump in the real interest rate.
cells.more:
The Phillips curve is `PI = lag(PI) + epsilon + gamma * (y - ys) / y`. When actual
output exceeds potential, inflation accelerates; when it falls short, inflation eases.
Fiscal policy reacts with `grG = gr - beta1 * (PI - lag(PI)) - beta2 * (lag(PI) - pi_target)`,
slowing (or speeding) the growth of pure spending `g` to steer inflation back to target
while accepting temporary output gaps.

cells.title: WP 494 fiscal reaction model (Section 2)

equations.name: r
equations.desc: Nominal interest rate — CB holds rr fixed (eq. 11-2).

equations.name: grG
equations.desc: Growth rate of pure govt spending (eq. 28).

equations.name: gT
equations.desc: Real total government outlays incl. interest (eq. 15).

equations.name: gd_y
equations.desc: Real government debt to real GDP.

cells.more:
Section 2 replaces eq. (13) with potential output `ys = lag(ys) * (1 + gr)` and
demand-led output `y = g + x` (eq. 21-2). Government spending follows eqs. (27–28)
instead of closing the resource gap directly. Inflation is endogenous (eq. 26); the
central bank holds `rr` fixed via `r = rr + PI + PI * rr` (eq. 11-2).

cells.title: FPRF externals

externals.name: gr
externals.desc: Real growth rate of productive potential (2.5%).

externals.name: alpha2
externals.desc: Speed of wealth adjustment / propensity out of wealth.

externals.name: alpha10
externals.desc: Autonomous propensity to spend from income.

externals.name: rr
externals.desc: Real interest rate held by the central bank (~1%).

cells.more:
Section 2 parameters mirror Table 2 baseline values plus the fiscal-reaction and
Phillips-curve coefficients used in the MODLER experiments (WP 494, Section 2).

cells.title: FPRF initial values
cells.more:
Starts from the Section 2 growing steady state: `ys = y = 100`, inflation at the
`2%` target, `g/y ≈ 26%`, and debt ratios near the Table 2 baseline.

cells.title: FPRF baseline steady state
cells.description: Section 2 baseline with fiscal reaction function at the 2% inflation target.

cells.title: Experiment 1 — Lower inflation target
cells.source:
Reproduces **Figures 2–4**: the inflation target `pi_target` is cut from `2%` to `1.5%`.
Fiscal policy slows the growth of `g`, creating a temporary output gap that brings
inflation down to the new target.

cells.title: Figure 3 — output gap after lower target
Figure 4 — growth of pure govt spending after lower target
Experiment 2 — Higher propensity to consume
cells.source:
Reproduces **Figure 5**: households raise `alpha10` from `0.9` to `0.95`, boosting
private spending. Fiscal policy responds by adjusting the growth rate of `g` to
contain the inflationary pressure.

cells.title: Figure 5 — output and fiscal spending growth after spending shock
Experiment 3 — Higher real interest rate
cells.source:
Reproduces **Figures 6–8**: the central bank raises the real interest rate `rr` from
`1%` to `7%`. Private spending falls; fiscal policy hikes the growth of `g` to
restore full employment, accepting a higher debt ratio (Figure 8).

cells.title: Figure 6 — inflation after real rate hike
Figure 7 — output and fiscal spending growth after rate hike
Figure 8 — debt ratio after real rate hike
Scenario 2 — Permanent trade deficit
cells.source:
Reproduces Figure 9 (Section 3): from period 5 onward imports exceed exports by 5%
(`trade_gap = 1.05`). With exports at 20% of GDP, the goods trade deficit is 1% of
GDP; the current account stabilizes near -2.5% of GDP and the budget deficit must be
higher by a corresponding amount.
cells.more:
**Section 3** opens the economy with exports growing at `gr` and imports fixed at
**`105%` of exports** (`trade_gap = 1.05`). Foreign wealth `VF` accumulates from the
current account `CAB`. Identity (D) becomes **`NAFA = DEFICIT + CAB`**: a country that
runs a chronic external deficit must run a larger budget deficit if private net lending
is to remain consistent with the flow-of-funds.

The paper's second surprising result: stability does **not** require a trade
**surplus** for a debtor country — the model converges with a sustained current-account
deficit near **`-2.5%` of GDP**, and the budget deficit ends up about **2.5 percentage
points higher** than in the closed economy (Figure 9).

cells.title: Permanent trade deficit
cells.more:
From period 5, `trade_gap = 1.05` imposes a permanent import excess. Real imports
`im` exceed exports `ex`, so `trade_bal` turns negative and eq. (21-2) requires
**higher `g`** for the same `y`. The current account **`CAB`** goes negative and
**`DEFICIT_Y`** rises relative to the closed-economy baseline — the twin-deficit
logic in stock-flow consistent form.

cells.title: Figure 9 — flow balances to GDP
cells.more:
Nominal government deficit, current account, private net lending, and the goods
trade balance as **percent of GDP**. With `trade_gap = 1.05` and `ex/y = 20%`, the
goods trade balance **`(EX - IM)/Y`** is **`-1%`**. As the trade gap opens, **`CAB/Y`**
and **`DEFICIT/Y`** move together, consistent with identity (D): **`NAFA/Y`**
tracks their sum.

In the paper's Figure 9, the current-account deficit stabilizes near **`-2.5%` of
GDP**; the nominal budget deficit is several percentage points of GDP higher than
with balanced trade. Use the baseline reference trace to see both deficits widen
jointly rather than offsetting each other.
